nativeRange.habitat: Clear coral reef flats and upper seaward slopes

decorPreferences: Mature live rock with grazing surfaces
Strong water flow and high dissolved oxygen

diet.notes: Predominantly grazes on benthic marine algae in the wild. Generally advised to offer dried nori/marine algae sheets via veggie clip multiple times per day, plus algae-based pellets and herbivore frozen formulations. A vitamin-rich, algae-heavy diet has been linked to reduced HLLE risk.

avoidWith: Other tangs and surgeonfish (especially Acanthurus species)
Small, timid fish that cannot compete at feeding time

imageCandidates.notes: Diego Delso, 4104x2736. Tack-sharp full-profile in-situ shot off Zanzibar; vivid powder-blue body, bright yellow dorsal band, black face mask, white throat and black-and-white/yellow tail all crisply shown against a blurred reef. Best all-round pick.
H. Zell, 4139x2771. Sharp, vibrant full-body shot in a natural reef setting with gorgonians; clear diagnostic markings, different composition from the recommended pick.
Ruben Undheim, 5184x3456. Very sharp, clean full lateral profile against a dark background; strong colour and complete fin detail, adds variety.

summary: The Powder Blue Tang is a striking Indian Ocean surgeonfish known for its vivid powder-blue body and yellow dorsal fin. It is widely regarded as a specialist-level species, being notoriously prone to marine ich and intensely aggressive toward other tangs.
careNotes: The Powder Blue Tang is widely regarded as one of the more demanding marine aquarium fish and is generally advised only for experienced keepers with large, mature systems. The species is notoriously prone to marine ich (Cryptocaryon irritans) and other ectoparasites; a strict quarantine protocol of at least four weeks, and ideally a tank-transfer or copper/chloroquine prophylactic regimen, is essentially mandatory before introduction. It is also susceptible to Head and Lateral Line Erosion (HLLE) when water quality slips or when diet lacks vitamin-rich marine algae. Behaviourally it is intensely aggressive toward conspecifics and other Acanthurus tangs; keep one per tank unless the system is genuinely very large. Together, these factors make it widely regarded as one of the more challenging tang species to keep successfully over the long term.
breedingNotes: Not bred in home aquaria. Wild reproduction involves paired or aggregation spawning with pelagic eggs and a long planktonic larval phase; captive breeding has not been documented at the hobby level.

additional.notes: Cross-checked taxonomy (Acanthuridae / Acanthuriformes), maximum size (54 cm TL wild, ~19 cm common), depth range (0-25 m), temperature preference (23-28°C), and Indian Ocean distribution.

additional.notes: Cross-checked typical aquarium adult size (~23 cm), tank size (~400 L), temperature (24-28°C), pH (8.0-8.5), specific gravity (1.023-1.025), reef-safe rating, and herbivorous diet.

additional.name: Reef Builders
additional.notes: Cross-checked ich susceptibility, mandatory quarantine, large-territory hyperaggression, and requirement for high dissolved oxygen and mature system.

fish.breedingNotes: Spawning has not been reported in home aquaria. In the wild, reproduction occurs through paired or group aggregation spawning, producing pelagic eggs that develop through a long planktonic larval phase.

hobbyistAdvisory.reason: Notoriously prone to marine ich and HLLE; adults reach around 25 cm and require a large, mature system of 700 L or more with mandatory quarantine. Generally advised only for experienced marine keepers.
